title: Board Diversity and Firm Risk: A Panel Study of S&P 500 Companies
authors: Harrington, J., Chen, L., and Osei, K.
abstract: This study examines the relationship between board gender diversity and total firm risk using a panel of S&P 500 firms from 2010 to 2020. We argue that greater board diversity reduces idiosyncratic ri
methods_data: We estimate a panel OLS regression of total firm risk (annualised return volatility) on the proportion of female board members, controlling for firm size, leverage, profitability, and board size. Firm fixed effects and year fixed effects are included to absorb unobserved heterogeneity. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level. The baseline specification is: Risk_it = alpha_i + gamma_t + beta * Diversity_it + X_it * delta + epsilon_it. As robustness checks we replace the dependent variable with systematic risk (market beta) and idiosyncratic risk separately, and we estimate the model using the within estimator to confirm results are not driven by between-firm variation.
results: The coefficient on board gender diversity is negative and statistically significant at the 1% level (beta = -0.042, SE = 0.011, p < 0.01), consistent with H1. Firm fixed effects account for the majority of variation in firm risk (R-squared within = 0.31). Robustness checks confirm the result is stable across alternative risk measures.
discussion: Our findings support agency-theoretic predictions that diverse boards engage in more conservative risk oversight. The effect is economically meaningful: a 10 percentage-point increase in female board representation is associated with a 0.42 standard deviation reduction in annualised return volatility.
footnotes: Data on board composition sourced from ISS Governance database. Financial data from Compustat.
full_text:
Board Diversity and Firm Risk: A Panel Study of S&P 500 Companies

Harrington, J., Chen, L., and Osei, K.

Abstract
This study examines the relationship between board gender diversity and total firm risk using a panel of S&P 500 firms from 2010 to 2020. We argue that greater board diversity reduces idiosyncratic ri

Methods and Data
We estimate a panel OLS regression of total firm risk (annualised return volatility) on the proportion of female board members, controlling for firm size, leverage, profitability, and board size. Firm fixed effects and year fixed effects are included to absorb unobserved heterogeneity. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level. The baseline specification is: Risk_it = alpha_i + gamma_t + beta * Diversity_it + X_it * delta + epsilon_it. As robustness checks we replace the dependent variable with systematic risk (market beta) and idiosyncratic risk separately, and we estimate the model using the within estimator to confirm results are not driven by between-firm variation.

Results
The coefficient on board gender diversity is negative and statistically significant at the 1% level (beta = -0.042, SE = 0.011, p < 0.01), consistent with H1. Firm fixed effects account for the majority of variation in firm risk (R-squared within = 0.31). Robustness checks confirm the result is stable across alternative risk measures.

Discussion
Our findings support agency-theoretic predictions that diverse boards engage in more conservative risk oversight. The effect is economically meaningful: a 10 percentage-point increase in female board representation is associated with a 0.42 standard deviation reduction in annualised return volatility.

Footnotes
Data on board composition sourced from ISS Governance database. Financial data from Compustat.
